meta.name: DES-II Dissociative Experiences Scale
meta.description: The DES-II is a 28-item self-report measure developed by Bernstein and Putnam to assess the frequency of dissociative experiences. It is widely used for screening dissociative disorders.
meta.author: Bernstein & Putnam (1986), Carlson & Putnam (1993)
meta.reference: Carlson EB, Putnam FW. An update on the Dissociative Experiences Scale. Dissociation. 1993;6(1):16-27.
meta.instruction: The following items describe experiences that some people have in daily life. Please indicate how often these experiences happen to you.

questions.text: Some people have the experience of driving or riding in a car and suddenly realizing that they don't remember what has happened during all or part of the trip
Some people find that sometimes they are listening to someone talk and they suddenly realize that they did not hear part or all of what was said
Some people have the experience of finding themselves in a place and having no idea how they got there
Some people have the experience of finding themselves dressed in clothes that they don't remember putting on
Some people have the experience of finding new things among their belongings that they do not remember buying
Some people sometimes find that they are approached by people that they do not know who call them by another name or insist that they have met them before
Some people sometimes have the experience of feeling as though they are standing next to themselves or watching themselves do something
Some people are told that they sometimes do not recognize friends or family members
Some people find that they have no memory for some important events in their lives (for example, a wedding or graduation)
Some people have the experience of being accused of lying when they do not think that they have lied
Some people have the experience of looking in a mirror and not recognizing themselves
Some people have the experience of feeling that other people, objects, and the world around them are not real
Some people have the experience of feeling that their body does not seem to belong to them
Some people have the experience of sometimes remembering a past event so vividly that they feel as if they were reliving that event
Some people have the experience of not being sure whether things that they remember happening really did happen or whether they just dreamed them
Some people have the experience of being in a familiar place but finding it strange and unfamiliar
Some people find that when they are watching television or a movie they become so absorbed in the story that they are unaware of other events happening around them
Some people find that they become so involved in a fantasy or daydream that it feels as though it were really happening to them
Some people find that they sometimes are able to ignore pain
Some people find that they sometimes sit staring off into space, thinking of nothing, and are not aware of the passage of time
Some people sometimes find that when they are alone they talk out loud to themselves
Some people find that in one situation they may act so differently compared with another situation that they feel almost as if they were two different people
Some people sometimes find that in certain situations they are able to do things with amazing ease and spontaneity that would usually be difficult for them
Some people sometimes find that they cannot remember whether they have done something or have just thought about doing that thing
Some people find evidence that they have done things that they do not remember doing
Some people sometimes find writings, drawings, or notes among their belongings that they must have done but cannot remember doing
Some people sometimes find that they hear voices inside their head that tell them to do things or comment on things that they are doing
Some people sometimes feel as if they are looking at the world through a fog so that people and objects appear far away or unclear

ranges.label: Normal Range
ranges.description: Your dissociative experiences fall within the normal range. Most people occasionally experience brief moments of absorption, daydreaming, or automatic behavior that are entirely typical and not clinically significant.
suggestions: These occasional dissociative experiences are a normal part of human cognition and attention
If certain experiences feel bothersome, note when and where they occur for self-awareness
Maintaining good sleep hygiene and stress management supports continued mental clarity

ranges.label: Mild Dissociative Experiences
ranges.description: Your dissociative experiences are mildly elevated above the population average. This may relate to ongoing stress, sleep deprivation, or anxiety, and warrants some self-monitoring to track changes over time.
suggestions: Monitor your stress levels and sleep quality, as both strongly influence dissociative experiences
Practice grounding and mindfulness exercises to strengthen present-moment awareness daily
Identify situations or triggers that seem to precede dissociative episodes for better self-understanding
If these experiences are increasing or affecting daily functioning, consult a mental health professional

ranges.label: Moderate Dissociative Experiences
ranges.description: Your dissociative experience frequency is clinically elevated and may indicate a dissociative disorder or related condition such as PTSD. A comprehensive professional evaluation is recommended to clarify the pattern.
suggestions: A professional evaluation by a clinician experienced with dissociative disorders is recommended
Learn about dissociative symptoms to better understand and communicate your experiences to others
Practice grounding techniques such as the 5-4-3-2-1 sensory exercise when feeling disconnected
Maintain regular daily routines and minimize controllable stressors to reduce dissociative episodes
Consider trauma-informed therapy approaches if your dissociation is linked to past traumatic events

ranges.label: Significant Dissociative Experiences
ranges.description: You report frequent and significant dissociative experiences. Scores at this level are strongly associated with dissociative disorders, complex PTSD, and related conditions requiring comprehensive clinical assessment.
suggestions: A thorough psychiatric or psychological evaluation by a dissociation specialist is strongly recommended
Dissociation often has roots in early traumatic experiences; trauma-focused therapy can be very effective
Develop a safety plan with your clinician for managing severe dissociative episodes in daily life
Do not underestimate the impact of these experiences on your safety, relationships, and daily functioning

amnesia.label: High
amnesia.description: Frequent amnesia-related dissociative experiences. Attention recommended.

depersonalization.label: High
depersonalization.description: Frequent depersonalization/derealization experiences. Professional evaluation recommended.

absorption.label: Moderate
absorption.description: Some absorption or imaginative involvement experiences.
